focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...a little boy used a donation pencil to draw a house on the back of a church bulletin, every line too dark, pressing hard enough to tear the paper at the corners. Tiana watched him do it until the outline gave way and the page ripped through the roof.

At lights-down, which was not darkness but a lower wattage of exposure, she lay on the cot with the email open once more because she did not yet know how to stop looking at the body. The canceled room. The canceled arrival. The sentence that turned a life into an option set. Around her, the hall settled by layers. Coughing. Whispering. A baby refusing sleep as a matter of principle. Somebody praying softly in patois near the bathrooms. Somebody else snoring with complete faith in exhaustion.

She thought of the road north she had not taken, not as some movie of what might have been. The truth was smaller and more embarrassing than that. She thought of small things: the first blast of dorm air-conditioning against her face after unloading a car; the sound of her own footsteps in a hallway where nobody knew her mother or her father's debts or her brother's mouth or the exact way floodwater smelled once it got into paper; the possibility of letting silence mean privacy instead of danger. What had closed was not education in the noble abstract. It was a room, a key, a first clean exit.

Beside her, Micah shifted on his cot and muttered once in his sleep. Across the hall, Delroy coughed, then went still. Tiana put the phone facedown on her chest and felt its heat through the thin church blanket.

Tomorrow would come with forms. Calls. Alternatives. The local path. The practical path. All the words people used when the original thing had already been cut away.

Tonight the loss was simpler than that and meaner.

She had stayed.
